failure_modes.description: Both routers claim ACTIVE for the same HSRP group, causing duplicate virtual IP and traffic black-holing

root_causes.cause: L2 issue preventing Hellos from reaching the other router
HSRP version mismatch (v1 vs v2 use different multicast addresses: 224.0.0.2 vs 224.0.0.102)

symptoms: show standby on both routers shows ACTIVE state for the same group
ARP table on hosts shows MAC flapping for the virtual IP
Intermittent connectivity for hosts using the virtual gateway

failure_modes.description: Higher priority router does not take over as ACTIVE after recovering, leaving a lower-priority router as ACTIVE

root_causes.cause: 'standby preempt' not configured (off by default in HSRP)
Preempt delay configured and delay period has not expired
Interface tracking decrementing priority below active router's priority (tracking an upstream failure)

symptoms: Higher-priority router is in STANDBY but should be ACTIVE
show standby shows higher priority on standby router than active router
After recovery, original active remains as standby

messages.name: Hello
messages.when_sent: Every hello-timer by active and standby routers

messages.description: Announces that sender is taking over as active (preemption)
messages.name: Coup
messages.when_sent: When a higher-priority router with preemption wants to become active

messages.description: Active router voluntarily gives up active role
messages.name: Resign
messages.when_sent: When active router is shutting down or being preempted

purpose: Hot Standby Router Protocol — provides default gateway redundancy (Cisco-specific)

states.description: HSRP starting, waiting for config or interface up
states.name: INIT

states.description: Waiting to hear from active router (no virtual IP known yet)
states.name: LEARN

states.description: Knows virtual IP, neither active nor standby. Normal steady state for routers that are not the active or standby router.
states.name: LISTEN

states.description: Candidate to become active if the active router fails
states.name: STANDBY

timers.name: hello-timer
timers.tuning_advice: Lower for faster failover detection. Millisecond timers available with 'standby timers msec'. Common aggressive setting: 200ms hello / 750ms hold.

timers.name: hold-timer
timers.tuning_advice: Must be >= 3x hello-timer. Lower for faster failover. Both active and standby must agree on timer values (advertised in Hello messages).

timers.description: Internal timers tracking active and standby router liveness
timers.name: active-timer/standby-timer
timers.tuning_advice: Not directly configurable. Controlled by the hold-timer values received in Hello messages from the active and standby routers.

transitions.trigger: No standby router detected and this router has highest priority among listeners
No active router detected and standby timer expires

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Active still reachable but hellos not arriving

transitions.trigger: Received higher priority Hello with preemption (or 'standby preempt' on higher-priority peer)
Interface shut down or HSRP removed

notes.note: HSRP available without feature enable. HSRPv1 default; 'standby version 2' for HSRPv2. Supports BFD-triggered failover.
HSRP supported but VRRP is generally preferred on IOS-XR. Configured under 'router hsrp' with interface-level groups.
HSRP requires 'feature hsrp'. HSRPv2 is default. Supports object tracking for priority adjustment. HSRP commonly used with vPC for L3 gateway redundancy.

CRITICAL DESIGN: HSRP active gateway and STP root bridge MUST be aligned per VLAN. If HSRP active is Switch A but STP root is Switch B, traffic hits Switch A (gateway) then traverses the peer-link/ISL to reach Switch B (root) — wasting bandwidth and adding latency. Set 'spanning-tree vlan <X> priority 4096' and 'standby <group> priority 110' + 'standby <group> preempt' on the same switch per VLAN.
